matuww

Writing Scripts Descended from Egyptian Hieroglyphs

× About Glossary Credits
☰


Latin

Alphabet1,2
4 c. BCE - Present1,2


Right-to-left1,2

Languages1,2

Latin and Romance languages (Italian, Spanish, Portuguese, Catalan, French, Romanian, etc.),
Germanic languages (English, German, Dutch, Icelandic, Norwegian, Swedish, Danish, etc.),
Celtic languages (Irish Gaellic, Welsh, Breton, Scottish Gaelic, Cornish, etc.),
Slavic languages (Polish, Czech, Slovak, Slovene, Serbo-Croatian, etc.), Baltic languages (Lithuanian, Latvian, etc.), Albanian,
Uralic languages (Finnish, Estonian, Hungarian, etc.), Altaic languages (Turkish, Uzbek, Kazakh, etc.), Basque, Maltese,
and languages in Africa (Swahili, Xhosa, Zulu, Hausa, Yoruba, etc.), Americas (Navajo, Nahuatl, Quechua languages, etc.), Asia (Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Tagalog, etc.), and Oceania (Maori, Hawaiian, etc.).

Places?1

Worldwide

Evolution1-6

Maturing alphabet
Archaic Latin

Claudian Classical Latin letter Antisigma
×

Claudian Classical Latin letter Antisigma

ↃC

bs, ps
IPA: /bz/, /ps/

Perhaps derived from variant of Archaic Greek letter Psi.

Claudian Classical Latin letter Digamma Inversum
×

Claudian Classical Latin letter Digamma Inversum

Ⅎ

Inversed Digamma

v
IPA: /w/

Perhaps derived from variant of Archaic Greek letter Wau.

Claudian Classical Latin letter H Dimidia
×

Claudian Classical Latin letter H Dimidia

Ⱶ

Half H

i, u
IPA: /ɨ ~ ʉ/

Y and Z were reintroduced to the Latin alphabet following the Roman conquest of the Hellenistic world.
3 letters were created by Emperor Claudius (r. 41 - 54 CE) and were eponymously named after him.
They were used in a limited extent and were abandoned after his death.

 
 

Classical Antiquity

Roman Capitals

Capitals are angular letters with straight lines with each letter individually formed distinctly and clearly separated from the others.
Capitals are majuscule.

Roman Square Capitals

Capitalis Quadrata

 ??? - 5 c. CE

a

Roman Square Capital letter Zéta

Z

z

Derived from Classical Latin letter Zéta (Ζ).

Square Capitals used in manuscrupts are derived from inscriptions carved on hard materials, such as marble.
While having a reputable impression and being only used in high-grade manuscripts, it is extremely time consuming to write in this hand.

Capitalis Rustica

This less rigid hand allowed scribes to write more rapidly than with Square Capitals.

 

Roman Cursives

Roman Cursives were used in day-to-day scenarios by Romans for secular use.
Note that these were highly non-standardised and letter shapes vary greatly.
Old Roman Cursive

Antiqua Cursiva Romana

 Before 1 c. BCE  - 5 c. CE

×

a

Old Roman Cursive letter Zéta

Old Roman Cursive letter Zéta

Z

z

Derived from Roman Square Capital letter Zéta (Ζ).

Old Roman Cursives were derived from Square Capitals.
While a cursive script, letters were more discerete than connected continuously, suggesting it was used to write on wax tablets than on papyrus.

New Roman Cursive

Nova Cursiva Romana

 3 c. CE - 7 c. CE

New Roman Cursive letter V Duplex
×

New Roman Cursive letter V Duplex

W

Double V

w

Derived from digraph of Latin letters V (V) and V (V)
in 7-8 c. CE for Germanic languages.

New Roman Cursive letter Ix
×

New Roman Cursive letter Ix

X

ks

Derived from Old Roman Cursive letter Ix (X).

Numerical value: 10

New Roman Cursive letter I Graeca
×

Y

y

Derived from Old Roman Cursive letter I Graeca (Υ).

New Roman Cursive letter Zéta
×

New Roman Cursive letter Zéta

Z

z

Derived from Old Roman Cursive letter Zéta (Ζ).

New Roman Cursives were derived from Old Roman Cursives.
As wax tablets as a writing medium went out of fashion, New Roman Cursive developed to become more rapidly written with connected letters.

 
 

Late Antiquity

Uncials

Uncials were a form of majuscule script. As parchment became more commonly used than papyrus, scribes were able to produce more rounded letterforms.
This hand was likely influenced by Greek Uncials, which are also characterised by their rounded shapes.

Roman Uncials

Uncialis

 3 c. CE  - 10 c. CE
Roman Uncial letter Á
×

Roman Uncial letter Á

A

a

Likely derived from New Roman Cursive letter Á (A).

b

Likely derived from New Roman Cursive letter Zéta (Ζ).

Roman Uncials were likely influenced by New Roman Cursives.
As the Roman Empire underwent Christianisation, Latin uncials grew in popularity, gradually replaced Roman Rustic Capitals, which had been commonly used for pagan literature, and became a favoured script for writing Christian texts.

Roman Half-uncials

Semiuncialis

 5 c. CE - 8 c. CE

Roman Half-uncial letter Zéta

Roman Half-uncial letter Zéta

Z

Likely derived from New Roman Cursive letter Zéta (Ζ).

Despite the name, Roman Half-uncials were not likely to be derived from Roman Uncials, but were likely influenced by New Roman Cursives as well.

 
 

Post-Roman National Hands

Following the collapse of the Roman Empire, there was a lack of a central authority to maintain the consistency of writing across its territories.
As a result, different regions began to develop their own distinct regional styles of writing.

Continental National Hands

The continental national hands were influcenced by New Roman Cursives.

Merovingian Minuscule letter Z

Merovingian Minuscule letter Z

z

Derived from New Roman Cursive letter Zéta (Ζ).

Orginated from France under the Merovingian dynasty. This is an umbrella term that encompasses a variety of scripts used in the region.
The example above is more specifically the "Luxeuil minuscule", eponymous of the Luxeuil Abbey in Burgundy, a prominent centre of writing and learning.

Originated from Iberia under the Visigothic Kingdom.
While based on New Roman Cursives, it shared influences from Roman Uncials and Half-unicals.

Beneventan Minuscule letter Z
×

Beneventan Minuscule letter Z

z

z

Derived from New Roman Cursive letter Zéta (Ζ).

Originated in southern Italy under the Duchy of Benevento. The Italian writing tradition survived in the south as the north was under Beneventan influence.
This was the most long-lived post-Roman national hand.

 
Insular National Hands

Meanwhile the national hands of the British Isles were more influenced by Roman Half-Uncials than New Roman Cursives.

Insular Majuscule (Rounded Hand) letter Z
×

Insular Majuscule (Rounded Hand) letter Z

z

z

Derived from Roman Half-uncial letter Zéta (Ζ).

Originated from Ireland, when catholics were first proselytising the island.
Also known as Insular Half-uncials or Rounded Hand. Manuscripts have characteristically been decorated with Celtic artistic tradition.
Later, this hand spreaded to the rest of the British Isles.

Insular Minuscule (Pointed Hand) letter Æsh
×

Insular Minuscule (Pointed Hand) letter Æsh

æ

ae

Derived from digraph of Insular Minuscule (Pointed Hand) letter A (a) and E (e).

Originated from Ireland and derived from Insular Majuscules.
Also known as Pointed Hand, this hand quickly replaced the Round Hand as it was more convenient to write and cursive. This hand is still used in Ireland today.
Meanwhile in England, this hand was used to write Old English. Addional modified Latin letters and letters derived from Anglo-Saxon Runes were added to the alphabet to represent sounds not found in Latin.

 
 

Carolingian Renaissance

The ambitious Charlemagne the Great promoted a standardise script across his empire to promote education. As a result, manuscripts were copied faster and with fewer mistakes.
This helped promote a rapid spread of literature, art, and science across Western Europe, ultimately driving the Carolingian Renaissance.

While developing from Roman Half-uncials, it was also received influences from the Merovingian, Visigothic, and Anglo-Saxon hands.

 
 

Gothic Minuscules

Not to be confused with the Gothic script used to write the Eastern Germanic Gothic language.
Gothic Minuscules were derived from Carolingian Minuscules. Its earlier form was known as Protogothic, which was visually similar to the latter.
As Gothic Minuscules developed, different categories and many regional variants also emerged.

Formal Gothic (Textualis)

Formal Gothic was derived from Protogothic in northern France in 12 c. CE. This began to be widely used in France, the Low Countries, England, and Germany.
There are four grades of Formal Gothic: Prescissa, Quadrata, Semiquadrata, and Rotunda.

Prescissa (sine pedibus) were characterised with angular letters lacking small decorative strokes at their extremities (serifs).

z

Derived from Gothic Prescissa Minuscule letter Z (z).

Quadrata was developed from Prescissa. It was characterised for having small square shaped serifs.

Semiquadrata shared a similar appearance to Quadrata but has less consistent serifs.

×

Gothic Rotunda Minuscule letter Z

z

z

Not to be confused with northern Italian variant of Gothic, also known as Rotunda.
This hand had upstroking curves at the bottom of letters, presumably to make writing faster.

 
Cursive Gothic (Littera Gothica Cursiva)

Cursive Gothic was developed from Formal Gothic in 13 c. CE.
It was likely due to the introduction of paper, which was smoother than parchment, that allowed for faster and more cursive writing.

Cursive Gothic was not standardised and many regional variants existed.

 
Bastard/Hybrid Gothic (Littera Gothica Bastarda/Hybrida)

Bastard Gothic was developed in 14 c. CE as from Formal Gothic and Cursive Gothic.

Developed from French Bastard Gothic (Lettre Bâtarde). This was widely used in Germany and Switzerland.

Influenced by Schwabacher letter Z (z).

A typeface commissioned Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I. It is named for its broken character of lines in the letters (Latin: fractus).
Outside of Germany, Fraktur was also used in Scandinavia, the Netherlands, Switzerland, and Austria at its peak.

 
 

Humanistic Script

During the Italian Renaissance, humanists created a reactionary script to Gothic by looking into the past, from what they thought was a "classical" script.
Thus they created the Humanistic Script.

Also known as Antiqua. This hand was derived from Caroline Minuscules.
Many printing typefaces and modern computer fonts were based on this.

Modern Latin1,28,30-51

As the use of the Latin script spread, regional variants were adopted by modifiying the Latin script for local languages.

Celtic (Indo-European)

Slavic (Indo-European)

Baltic (Indo-European)

Uralic

Epigraphy, Paleography, and Typography

Classical Antiquity

Archaic Latin27

Lapis Niger (Black Stone). 5 c. BCE. Rome, Italy.
This stone perhaps marked the grave of Romulus, the first king of Rome.
One of the earliest Latin inscriptions. (Description - here)


Roman Capitals

Roman Square Capitals54

Inscriptions on Trajan's Column. 113 CE. Rome, Italy.
A Roman triumphal column commemorating Roman emperor Trajan's victory in the Dacian Wars. (Description and translation - here)


Roman Rustic Capitals55

Electorial graffiti. Pompeii, Italy.


Roman Cursives

Old Roman Cursive56

Papyrus Berlin 8507. c. 41 - 51 CE.
Contained portions of speeches delivered in the Roman Senate. (Description and transliteration - here)


New Roman Cursive57

A call up notice served on an inhabitant of Hermapolis. Egypt. 6 c. CE. (Description - here)



Late Antiquity

Uncials

Roman Uncials58

Pal. lat. 210. Likely Italy. 6 - 7 c. CE.
Manuscript collection of early Christian theological opuscule texts (mainly works of St. Augustine and related writers). (View - here)


Roman Half-uncials59

Veterum Fragmentorum Manuscriptis Codicibus detractorum collectio Tom. II. Northern Italy. c. 5 - 10 CE.
Collection containing the oldest Vulgate version of the Gospels and fragments of Psalm manuscripts in Latin and German.
(Description - here)



Continental National Hands

East-Frankish Minuscules60

Chronica / Fredegar, VLQ 5. Switzerland. 8 - 9 c. CE.
Chronicle of Fredegar, a 7th century Frankish chronicle. (View - here)


Merovingian Minuscules61

Lectionarius gallicanus. Likely Luxeuil Abbey, France. 7 - 8 c. CE.
Contains the readings for several feast days, the Prophetical Lessons, and epistles and Gospels. (Description - here)


Visigothic Minuscules62

The 'Silos Apocalypse'. Santo Domingo de Silos, Spain. c. 1091-1109 CE.
Commentary on the Apocalypse (Description - here)


Beneventan Minuscules63

BPL 2842. Monte Cassino Abbey, Italy. 11 c. CE. (Description - here)


Insular National Hands

Insular Majuscules (Rounded Hand)64

The Book of Kells. Columban monasteries in Ireland and Britain. 9 c. CE.
Contains the four Gospels in Latin. (Description and view - here)


Insular Minuscules (Pointed Hand)65

The Exeter Book. England. c. 10 c. CE.
Codex of Old English poetry. (Description and view - here)



Caroline Minuscules66

Cod. Sang. 116. Switzerland. 9 c. CE.
Collection of Christian theological and exegetical texts. (Description and view - here)



Gothic Minuscules

Proto-Gothic67

Rochester New Testament. Rochester Cathedral Priory, England. c. 1130 CE. (Description and view - here)


Formal Gothic

Gothica Textura Prescissa68

The Luttrell Psalter. England. c. 1320 – 1340 CE. (Description - here)


Gothica Textura Quadrata69

Canon Missae. Mainz, Germany. 1458 CE. (Description - here)


Gothica Textura Rotunda70

Exerpt from a Book of Hours in Dutch. Likely Utrecht, Netherlands. 15 c. CE. (Description and translation - here)


Cursive Gothic?7

Speculum humanae salvationis. France or Low Countries. 15 c. CE.
The Mirror of Human Salvation - contains the Old Testament, New Testament, 7 Stations of the Passion, 7 Sorrows and 7 Joys of Mary. (Description and view - here)


Bastard Gothic71

Book of fayttes of armes and of Chyualrye. 11 - 16 c. CE.
Written in Burgundian bâtarde (Lettre Bourguignone). (Description - here)


English Bastard Gothic72

The Regiment of Princes. London, England. c. 1411 - 1420 CE. (Description and view - here)



Schwabacher73

The Nuremberg Chronicle. Germany. 1493 CE. (View - here)


Fraktur74

Prayer book of Maximilian I. Augsburg, Germany. 1513 CE. (Description in German - here)



Humanist

Humanist Minuscules75

Eusebius Caesariensis, Prosper de Aquitania, Chronicon. Florence, Italy. 15 c. CE. (Description and view - here)


Humanist Cursives76

Conventi soppressi J.VI.11. Italy. 15 c. CE.
Written by Niccolò Niccoli, an Italian Renaissance humanist, whose cursive script developed into Italic type. (Description - here)



Roman Type77

De evangelica praeparatione. Venice, Italy. 1470 CE.
Printed by Nicholas Jenson. (Description - here)



Italic Type78

Eclogues. Venice, Italy. 1501 CE.
Printed bu Aldus Manutius. (Description - here)



Classical Typefaces

Venetian Typefaces79-80


Garalde Typefaces81-83


Transitional Typefaces84-87



Modern Typefaces

Didone Typefaces87-89


Mechanistic Typefaces90-92


Lineal Typefaces

Grotesque Typefaces93-95

Neo-Grotesque Typefaces96-98

Geometric Typefaces99-101

Humanist Typefaces102-104


First 3 stanzas from
Luceafărul (The Morning Star)
by Mihai Eminescu in Romanian105-107

Romanian

A fost odată ca-n povești,
A fost ca niciodată,
Din rude mari împărătești,
O prea frumoasă fată.

Și era una la părinți
Și mândră-n toate cele,
Cum e Fecioara între sfinți
Și luna între stele.

Din umbra falnicelor bolți
Ea pasul și-l îndreaptă
Lângă fereastră, unde-n colț
Luceafărul așteaptă.

Translation

There was, as in the fairy tales,
As ne'er in the time's raid,
There was, of famous royal blood
A most beautiful maid.

She was her parents' only child,
Bright like the sun at noon,
And like the virgin midst the saints
And among stars the moon.

From the deep shadow of the vaults
Her step now she directs
Toward a window: at its nook
Bright Lucifer expects.


Keyboards108




Latin QWERTY keyboard on Gboard.
The most commonly used Latin keyboard.


Latin AZERTY keyboard on Gboard.
More commonly used in France, Belgium, and Francophone countries.


Latin Dvorak keyboard on Gboard.
An alternative keyboard layout to type English.


Latin Colemak keyboard on Gboard.
An alternative keyboard layout to type English.


Script comparison1-3,109-126

Writing Script Head of ox House plan Throw stick Nile tilapia Man with arms raised Pear shaped mace Two diagonal strokes Plan of rectangular enclosure Heart and trachea Arm with downward facing palm Hand Crook Water ripple Cobra Reed column Eye Mouth Rush with shoots Cord on stick Head Bow Crossed sticks
Egyptian Hieroglyphs
 
Logography
            
32 c. BCE - 4 c. CE
Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. F1
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. F1

𓃾

Head of Ox

Ideogram for: kꜣ “cattle”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʀ/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʀ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʔ/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /kɑ/
Conventional Anglicisation: ka

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. O1
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. O1

𓉐

House plan

Phonogram pr for: pr “house”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpaːɾuw/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpaːɾuw/
Reconstructed Medio-Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpaːɾəʔ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /poːɾ/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /pɛr/
Conventional Anglicisation: per

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. T14
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. T14

𓌙

Throw stick

Determinative for “foreign” for: qmꜣ “create”
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /kɛmɑ/
Conventional Anglicisation: qema

Ideogram in: ꜥꜣm “Asiatics”
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /ɑːʔɑm/
Conventional Anglicisation: aam

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. K1

𓆛

Bulti fish

𓀠

Man with arms raised

Two diagonal strokes

Determinative for duality

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. 06
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. O6

𓉗

Plan of rectangular enclosure

Ideogram for: ḥwt “temple, tomb, enclosure”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈħaːwit/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈħaːwiʔ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈħoː(wə)/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /ħuːt/
Conventional Anglicisation: hut

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. F35

𓄤

Heart and windpipe

Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈnaːfiɾ/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈnaːfiʔ/
Reconstructed Medio-Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈnaːfə/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈnoːfə/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /nɛfɛr/
Conventional Anglicisation: nefer

𓂣

Arm with downward facing palm

Hand

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. D1
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. D1

𓁶

Head

Phonogram tp
Determinative or ideogram for: tp “head”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /tʼap/, or possibly /cʼap/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /tʼap/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /tʼap/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /tɛp/
Conventional Anglicisation: tepw

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. T9a
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. T9a

𓌓

Bow

Phonogram pd/pḏ for: pḏt “bow”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpiːcʼat/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpiːtʼaʔ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpiːtʼə/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /pɛd͡ʒɛt/
Conventional Anglicisation: pedjet

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. Z9
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. Z9

𓏴

Crossed sticks

Phonogram swꜣ for: swꜣ “pass”

Syllabary
c.1910 CE - c. 1937 CE

128. Albright, William F. (1966). The Proto-Sinaitic Inscriptions and their Decipherment.
129. Davidson, L. (2022, March 18). How the Phoenician Alphabet Revolutionised Language. Retrieved March 7, 2026, from historyhit.com website: https://www.historyhit.com/how-the-phoenician-alphabet-revolutionised-language/
130. Bakker, E. J. (Ed.). (2010). A companion to the ancient Greek language: Bakker/A companion to the ancient Greek language. Wiley-Blackwell.
